query: telomestatin mechanism of action and approval status for cholesterol treatment

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Dieting Approaches to Treatment of Obesity -- Issues of Concern -- III) Drugs approved by the FDA for the long-term treatment of obesity
retrieved_docs.content: The FDA approved 5 drugs for the long-term treatment of obesity in the general population, as listed below. All five drugs have been studied in detail with multiple trials for efficacy, safety, and cardiometabolic benefits as per the FDA mandate. Below is a detailed mechanism of action, year of FDA approval, important trials that proved their benefits, approximate weight loss, dosing, contraindications, precautions, adverse effects, and important facts related to each drug.

retrieved_docs.title: Antilipemic Agent Bile Acid Sequestrants -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Bile acid sequestrants, including colesevelam, colestipol, and cholestyramine, are approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) in combination with restriction of dietary saturated and trans-fatty acids to manage hypercholesterolemia. They are indicated for patients with hypercholesterolemia without hypertriglyceridemia. [1] Bile acid sequestrants are beneficial therapy in statin-intolerant patients (myalgia and myopathy). They can be used in combination with niacin and the cholesterol absorption inhibitor ezetimibe to achieve target goals in patients in primary and secondary prevention. They can lower LDL-C between 15 to 30% if patients tolerate it at full doses. Some bile acid sequestrants are also FDA-approved for adolescents (10 to 17 years of age). [2] Therefore, bile acid sequestrants are 1 of the few drugs safe for children with heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia.

retrieved_docs.title: T-Cell Lymphoma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Retinoids help through their antiproliferative and antiapoptotic properties. Commonly used topical regimens are bexarotene (FDA-approved) and tazarotene. Severe hyperlipidemia and central hypothyroidism are side effects of bexarotene. Systemic retinoids include acitretin, isotretinoin, and bexarotene. Topical chemotherapy commonly used are mechlorethamine (nitrogen mustard), an alkylating agent, and carmustine. Systemic chemotherapy commonly used is methotrexate, pralatrexate, chlorambucil, gemcitabine, pegylated doxorubicin, and antimetabolite like fludarabine phosphate, 2-deoxycoformycin. PUVA inhibits DNA and RNA synthesis, but it will affect both normal and neoplastic cells; thus, the side effect profile is secondary malignancies of the skin. Histone deacetylase inhibitors (HDACi) are the newer group of therapeutic options available for the treatment of CTCL, which have both transcription-dependent and independent mechanisms of action for the expression of tumor suppressor genes. Vorinostat and romidepsin are FDA-approved, with an overall response rate of almost 50% with a tolerable side effect profile, the prominent one being cardiac arrhythmias from QTc prolongation. Targeted therapies include imiquimod (Toll-like receptor agonist), denileukin diftitox (recombinant fusion protein), alemtuzumab (a monoclonal antibody against CD52), and everolimus ( mTOR pathway target). Agents that are still undertrial are proteasome inhibitors like bortezomib. Allogenic stem cell transplant is used in advanced stages, usually appropriate for young patients with relapsing disease. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Familial Hypertriglyceridemia -- Treatment / Management -- Statins
retrieved_docs.content: Olezarsen, administered as a monthly injection, has recently received a "fast-track" designation from the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for treating familial chylomicronemia and has shown promise in managing FHTG. It is a synthetically modified DNA fragment that targets hepatocyte mRNA to inhibit apolipoprotein C-III (APOC3) synthesis, thus enhancing the clearance of triglyceride-rich lipoproteins. Clinical trials have demonstrated a reduction in plasma triglyceride levels by approximately 60% after 6 months. [37] [38] [39] [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Lomitapide -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Lomitapide could be beneficial for HoFH patients exhibiting insufficient response to PCSK9 inhibitor treatment, those with atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease and a baseline LDL-C of 190 mg/dL or higher, and those who demonstrate insufficient responses to statins, whether used alone or in combination with PCSK9 inhibitors or ezetimibe. Lomitapide rapidly binds to and inhibits microsomal triglyceride transfer protein (MTP) within the endoplasmic reticulum lumen, which impedes the synthesis of lipoproteins containing apo-B in both hepatocytes and enterocytes. This process reduces the production of very low-density lipoprotein and chylomicrons, thereby lowering plasma LDL-C levels. The safety and efficacy of lomitapide have not been established for heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia. This activity elucidates the indications, mechanism of action, pharmacokinetics, adverse event profile, and other relevant interactions essential for interprofessional healthcare team members in treating patients with HoFH and related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Lipid-Lowering Drug Therapy -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Bile acid sequestrants bind with bile acids in the intestines, thus interrupting the reabsorption of bile acids. The ensuing reduction in the cholesterol pool lowers intrahepatic cholesterol, promoting the upregulation of LDL receptors, causing a further decrease in blood cholesterol. The bile acid sequestrants also induce a minimal elevation in high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL-C). [21] BAS can reduce LDL starting at 15 percent and up by 24 percent at the maximal recommended dose. [22] They can raise triglyceride levels and should not be used if TG is greater than 400 mg/dl.

retrieved_docs.title: Pitavastatin -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Pitavastatin is a medication used in the management and treatment of hypercholesterolemia and dyslipidemia. It is in the statin class of drugs. This activity reviews the indications, action, and contraindications for pitavastatin as a valuable agent in managing dyslipidemia. This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, and other key factors, including dosing, pharmacokinetics, monitoring, and relevant interactions pertinent for interprofessional team members in the management of patients with dyslipidemia and related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: PCSK9 Inhibitors -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Proprotein convertase subtilisin/kexin type 9 (PCSK9) has emerged as an important regulator of cholesterol metabolism. In the United States, 2 fully humanized monoclonal antibodies—alirocumab and evolocumab— have been approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) to inhibit or reduce PCSK9 activity. Recently, both the European Union and FDA have approved inclisiran, a small interfering mRNA, that inhibits intracellular PCSK9 synthesis. Increased PCSK9 activity correlates with heightened LDL-C levels, while specific gain-of-function mutations lead to autosomal dominant familial hypercholesterolemia characterized by extremely high total and low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels, premature atherosclerotic vascular disease, and the formation of tendon xanthomas. In most cases, this is due to a genetic mutation of the LDL receptor caused by mutations of the apoprotein B100 or PCSK9 genes. Conversely, individuals with reduced PCSK9 activity, whether due to genetic polymorphism or the administration of PCSK9 monoclonal antibodies, exhibit lower cholesterol levels and a reduced risk of cardiovascular disease. This activity outlines the indications, mechanism of action, administration methods, significant adverse effects, contraindications, monitoring, and toxicity of PCSK9 inhibitors. This activity also helps the interprofessional healthcare team tailor patient therapy for optimal outcomes in serum lipid management and improve cardiovascular outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Lipid-Lowering Drug Therapy -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Fibrates are agonists for the nuclear transcription factor peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor alpha (PPAR-alpha). This receptor downregulates apoprotein C-III (an inhibitor of lipoprotein lipase) while upregulating the synthesis of apolipoprotein A-I, lipoprotein lipase, and fatty acid transport protein, resulting in an increase in VLDL catabolism and elimination of triglyceride-rich particles; this results in lowering of total plasma triglycerides by 30% to 60%; also a modest increase in HDL has been observed.

retrieved_docs.title: Tretinoin -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Explain the importance of monitoring for patients on tretinoin therapy, including target triglyceride and liver function concentrations. Summarize the risks associated with initiating tretinoin treatment along with key patient counseling points. Identify the most common adverse events associated with tretinoin treatment. Outline the importance of how collaboration and coordination among the interprofessional team can enhance patient care when dosing and monitoring tretinoin to improve patient outcomes for patients receiving topical (anti-acne and palliative for photoaging) and oral (antineoplastic) tretinoin. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Statin Medications -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperlipidemia and mixed dyslipidemia [2] Primary dysbetalipoproteinemia (Type III hyperlipoproteinemia) [3] Hypertriglyceridemia [4] Atherosclerosis [5] Primary prevention of ASCVD (atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease) [6] Secondary prevention in patients with clinical ASCVD [7] Pediatric patients with familial hypercholesterolemia [8] Adult patients with homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Mipomersen(Archived) -- Indications -- FDA Approved Use
retrieved_docs.content: The U.S. Food and Drug Administration approves Mipomersen as an adjunct to current lipid-lowering therapy and diet modifications to limit low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C), apolipoprotein B-100 (apoB), and non-high-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol in patients diagnosed with homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia (HoFH). [1] [2] A combination of therapies may be utilized for patients unable to achieve their target low-density lipoprotein-cholesterol levels.

retrieved_docs.title: Familial Hypercholesterolemia -- Treatment / Management -- Drug Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: Statins are the standard therapy for familial hypercholesterolemia. All guidelines recommend statins as first-line drugs for patients with FH, with a goal of reaching maximally tolerated doses. [33] The effect of statins has been well studied, and most randomized clinical trials observed a reduction of around 50 percent of the initial untreated LDL levels, as well as a reduction in cardiovascular events. [34] [35] [36] Unfortunately, a large number of patients with FH, even on maximally-tolerated statins alone, either do not show this level of reduction in LDL levels, or they do not reach their goal LDL levels, even with a 50 percent reduction, because their initial LDL levels are so high. [35] Second-line therapies include ezetimibe and PCSK9 inhibitors. Ezetimibe has been showing to add an extra 10 to 30 percent reduction in LDL levels for patients on maximally tolerated statins. [37] [38] Currently, ezetimibe is the drug of choice as the second line in most guidelines. [39] PCSK9 inhibitors have shown a great reduction in LDL levels and cardiovascular outcomes in different randomized clinical trials. [22] [40] In those trials, PCKS9 inhibitors lowered LDL levels by 50 to 60 percent. While exhibiting higher efficacy than ezetimibe, the use of PCSK9 inhibitors is limited by their high cost and the reluctance of insurance companies to approve their use. [41] Hence, PCSK9 inhibitors can be used as second or third-line drugs for patients with familial hypercholesterolemia. Fourth line therapies include mipomersen (mRNA inhibition of apolipoprotein B), lomitapide (microsomal triglyceride transfer protein inhibition), niacin, lipoprotein apheresis, ileal bypass surgery, and liver transplantation. [42] [43] [44] [45] These are usually reserved if the LDL does not reach the target level using statins, ezetimibe, and PCSK9 inhibitors. [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Lecithin-Cholesterol Acyltransferase Deficiency -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: There is no definitive treatment available for LCAT deficiency. Instead, the primary focus of management revolves around addressing its complications. This involves implementing dietary modifications, exercise, lipid-lowering therapies, and antihypertensive medications, particularly angiotensin-converting inhibitors (ACE-Is) or angiotensin receptor blockers (ARBs). These measures constitute the cornerstone of treatment and have demonstrated efficacy in slowing disease progression. Additionally, corticosteroid therapy has been proposed as a potentially advantageous option. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Statin Medications -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Statin medications are used in the management of hypercholesteremia. Statins are inhibitors of hydroxymethylglutaryl-CoA reductase enzyme and lower total cholesterol, low-density lipoprotein (LDL), and triglyceride concentrations. The FDA-approved statins include atorvastatin, rosuvastatin, simvastatin, pravastatin, fluvastatin, lovastatin, and pitavastatin. Individuals at high risk for atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease require more aggressive management strategies, including higher-intensity statin therapy. Participating clinicians review the mechanisms of action, dosing protocols, clinical toxicology, potential drug interactions, pharmacokinetics, and monitoring strategies pertinent to healthcare professionals in managing hypocholesteremia. The utility of coronary artery calcium scoring in situations of uncertainty is emphasized. This activity informs the interprofessional team on the best prescribing guidelines for statins.

retrieved_docs.title: Antilipemic Agent Bile Acid Sequestrants -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Colesevelam: Three double-blind, placebo-controlled trials with the use of colesevelam HCL (COL) in patients with diabetes mellitus type 2 (T2D) patients showed reductions in LDL-C and hemoglobin (Hb)A1c. These results led to the FDA approval of COL as an adjuvant to exercise and dietary restriction for improving glycemic control in patients with T2D. Therefore, COL therapy is effective for patients with diabetes who have not met their HbA1c goal of below 7% or LDL-C goal of under 100 mg/dL or 30 to 49 %  or over 50 % reduction in LDL-C. [2] The main advantage of using bile acid sequestrants is that they are safe for administration combined with other anti-diabetic medications and statins. They are the safest LDL-C lowering therapy to date.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2772
retrieved_docs.content: Bile acid sequestrants are a class of lipid‑lowering agents that function by binding bile acids in the intestine, thereby reducing cholesterol reabsorption and prompting the liver to convert more cholesterol into bile acids. Two commonly used members of this class are colestipol and colesevelam, both of which share the same mechanism of action and are employed to help manage hypercholesterolemia, particularly when statin therapy alone is insufficient or contraindicated. By decreasing low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels, these agents contribute to cardiovascular risk reduction and are an important therapeutic option in comprehensive dyslipidemia management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: Bile acid sequestrants are a class of lipid‑lowering agents that function by binding bile acids in the intestine, thereby reducing cholesterol absorption and lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) levels. Both colestipol and kolesevelam are specific drugs that belong to this class, illustrating that they share the same mechanism of action and therapeutic purpose within the broader category of bile acid sequestrants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2538
retrieved_docs.content: Inclisiran and evolocumab are both classified as members of the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “Other lipid modifying agents.” This classification indicates that each drug is used to modify lipid concentrations, particularly low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol, and is employed in the treatment of hyperlipidaemia and the reduction of cardiovascular risk. By belonging to the same ATC category, inclisiran and evolocumab share a therapeutic purpose of lowering lipid levels, although they may differ in their specific mechanisms of action and dosing regimens. Recognizing them as part of the “Other lipid modifying agents” group helps clinicians understand their role within the broader spectrum of lipid‑lowering therapies and supports informed decision‑making in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1225
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin are all classified as members of the therapeutic group “HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs.” This class of agents inhibits the enzyme HMG CoA reductase, thereby reducing hepatic cholesterol synthesis and lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels. As plain lipid‑modifying drugs, these statins are used to manage hyperlipidaemia and reduce cardiovascular risk in patients with elevated cholesterol. Recognizing that fluvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin share this mechanism of action and therapeutic category helps clinicians select appropriate statin therapy based on individual drug properties, efficacy, and safety profiles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1085
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, fluvastatin and rosuvastatin are all members of the class of HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This drug class works by inhibiting the HMG CoA reductase enzyme, the rate‑limiting step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis, leading to reduced low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels and decreased risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. As plain lipid‑modifying agents, these statins are commonly prescribed for the treatment of hyperlipidaemia and for primary and secondary prevention of cardiovascular events. While they share the same mechanism of action, each agent differs in potency, pharmacokinetic properties and individual safety profiles, allowing clinicians to tailor therapy to the specific needs of each patient.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1293
retrieved_docs.content: Bile acid sequestrants are classified as plain lipid‑modifying agents, a drug class used to lower cholesterol levels. Colesevelam is a specific medication that belongs to the bile acid sequestrant class. Because of its pharmacologic properties, colesevelam is contraindicated in patients who have الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياس, and therefore should not be prescribed to individuals with this disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1281
retrieved_docs.content: Colesevelam is classified as a bile acid sequestrant, a group of non‑absorbable polymeric resins that bind bile acids in the intestinal lumen and prevent their reabsorption. By interrupting the enterohepatic circulation of bile acids, colesevelam promotes hepatic conversion of cholesterol into new bile acids, which results in a reduction of low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels. In addition to its lipid‑lowering effect, colesevelam has been shown to modestly improve glycemic control in patients with type 2 diabetes, making it a useful adjunct therapy for individuals who require both cholesterol management and blood‑glucose regulation. Common adverse effects are gastrointestinal, such as constipation, flatulence, and abdominal discomfort, reflecting the drug’s mechanism of action within the gut. Because colesevelam does not undergo systemic absorption, it has a low potential for drug‑drug interactions, although it may interfere with the absorption of other oral medications and fat‑soluble vitamins, necessitating appropriate timing of administration. Overall, colesevelam exemplifies the therapeutic role of bile acid sequestrants in cardiovascular risk reduction and metabolic disease management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 262
retrieved_docs.content: sulfonylureas (glibenclamide, glimepiride, tolbutamide; torasemide is not a sulfonylurea), biguanides (fenformin/metformin), DPP‑4 inhibitors (alogliptin, sitagliptin, and the fixed‑dose combination simvastatin/sitagliptin), and GLP‑1 analogues (exenatid, liraglutide, lixisenatide). Fixed‑dose combinations that pair basal insulins with GLP‑1 analogues—insulin degludec/liraglutide and insulin glargine/lixisenatide—illustrate contemporary strategies to enhance glycaemic control. Growth‑hormone‑based therapies are represented by somatropin and its agonists, including mecasermin, sermorelin, somapacitan and tesamorelin, which function as growth‑hormone analogues or secretagogues. The thyrotropin class contains thyreotropin alfa, a synthetic thyroid‑stimulating hormone analogue used diagnostically and therapeutically. Collectively, these hierarchical classifications link specific compounds to their mechanisms, chemical families, and clinical applications, providing a structured framework

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1978
retrieved_docs.content: Bezafibrate is classified as a member of the fibrates, a group of plain lipid‑modifying drugs. As part of this drug class, bezafibrate shares the typical pharmacologic actions of fibrates, which are used to alter lipid profiles by lowering triglyceride levels and modestly increasing high‑density lipoprotein cholesterol.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1004
retrieved_docs.content: Bezafibrát, klofibrát, fenofibrát and gemfibrozil are all members of the class “Fibrates, lipid modifying drugs, plain,” indicating that each of these agents functions as a fibrate used to modify lipid metabolism. Klofibrát specifically produces the physiologic effect of decreased lipolysis, reflecting its mechanism of reducing the breakdown of triglycerides. Gemfibrozil is also categorized under the preparation group “G [Preparations],” linking it to a broader formulation category. In terms of drug interactions, gemfibrozil is contraindicated when used with repaglinide, meaning that concurrent administration of these two agents should be avoided. Repaglinide itself belongs to the class “Other blood glucose lowering drugs, excl. insulins in ATC,” identifying it as a non‑insulin oral hypoglycemic agent. The relationship between fibrates (lipid‑lowering drugs) and repaglinide (a glucose‑lowering drug) highlights the importance of recognizing potential contraindications, such as that between gemfibrozil and repaglinide, to ensure safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1624
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, fluvastatin and pitavastatin are all members of the therapeutic class identified as HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This class of agents works by inhibiting the HMG CoA reductase enzyme, which is the rate‑limiting step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis, thereby lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and reducing overall cardiovascular risk. Because they share the same mechanism of action, these statins are commonly used in the management of hyperlipidaemia and for primary or secondary prevention of atherosclerotic disease. Recognizing that atorvastatin, fluvastatin and pitavastatin belong to the same drug class helps clinicians select appropriate lipid‑lowering therapy and anticipate class‑related effects and potential drug interactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 0 -- Community 1
retrieved_docs.content: The ATC system groups antidiabetic and lipid‑modifying therapies into a hierarchical framework that links drug classes, mechanisms of action, molecular targets, and clinical considerations. Insulins and analogues for injection—including rapid‑acting insulin aspart, intermediate‑ or long‑acting insulins such as insulin glargine and beef insulin, and fixed‑dose combinations insulin degludec / liraglutide and insulin glargine / lixisenatide—belong to the “insulins‑and‑analogues” class and lower glucose partly by decreasing lipolysis through activation of the human insulin‑receptor (INSR). Non‑insulin blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs are organized under “other blood glucose‑lowering drugs, excl. insulins” and comprise several subclasses: • Alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors (unspecified agents). • Biguanides, the “biguanide blood glucose lower drugs” subclass, represented by metformin and fenformin (phenformin). • Meglitinides such as repaglinide. • Thiazolidinediones/antilipemic agents including

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 233
retrieved_docs.content: Antilipaemica en geneesmiddelen tegen arteriosclerose is defined as an anti‑atherosclerotic therapeutic group and includes niacinamide (niacinamid), which is used to treat elevated blood triglyceride levels (ارتفاع نسبة الدهون الثلاثية في الدم) and to prevent coronary artery disease (مرض الشريان التاجي). In lipid metabolism, medium‑chain triglycerides are contraindicated in patients with elevated triglycerides, underscoring the need to select appropriate lipid‑modifying agents. Within the family of sex‑hormone modulators, the structural classes Estren‑derivatives, Pregnadien‑derivatives, and Pregnen‑4‑derivatives are all classified as progestogen sex hormones and modulators of the genital system. Specific agents placed in these classes include: * **Lynestrenol** – an Estren‑derivative that belongs to progestogen systemic hormonal contraceptives, is associated with the norethindrone family of progestins, and is used in systemic hormonal contraception. * **Dienogest** – a

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1460
retrieved_docs.content: Bezafibrate and fenofibrate are both classified as fibrates, a group of plain lipid‑modifying drugs. As members of the fibrate class, these agents are used to alter lipid metabolism, typically to lower triglyceride levels and raise high‑density lipoprotein cholesterol, thereby contributing to the management of dyslipidaemia. Their shared classification underscores their similar pharmacologic actions and therapeutic role in cardiovascular risk reduction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2462
retrieved_docs.content: HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid modifying drugs constitute a therapeutic class of statin medications that lower cholesterol by inhibiting the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme in the hepatic cholesterol synthesis pathway. Atorvastatin, fluvastatin, and pravastatin are individual agents that belong to this class. By reducing low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and modestly increasing high‑density lipoprotein (HDL), these drugs are employed to manage hyperlipidemia and to lower the risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Their shared mechanism of action and lipid‑modifying effects make them central components of preventive cardiology and patient care strategies aimed at controlling dyslipidemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2659
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin and fluvastatin are members of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This drug class works by inhibiting the enzyme HMG‑CoA reductase, the rate‑limiting step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis, which leads to reduced low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels and a lower risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. As members of the same pharmacologic group, atorvastatin and fluvastatin share similar indications for treating hypercholesterolemia and for primary and secondary prevention of cardiovascular events, as well as comparable safety considerations such as the potential for myopathy and drug‑drug interactions. Recognizing both agents as part of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor class helps clinicians apply class‑wide prescribing guidelines while selecting a specific statin based on potency, pharmacokinetic profile, and individual patient tolerance.
